text:
The process began with a 45-minute phone screen featuring a string manipulation problem that
evolved into a sliding window challenge. The onsite consisted of four technical rounds and one
"Googliness" round. The technical problems were heavily focused on graphs—specifically
finding the shortest path in a 2D grid with obstacles and implementing a custom iterator for a
nested list. One round pushed for a O(N) solution where a O(N log N) sorting approach
seemed obvious, forcing me to think about frequency arrays. Takeaway: Communication is as
vital as the code. Google interviewers want to see how you react to constraints like "What if the
data is too large for memory?" Be vocal about your trade-offs between time and space
complexity, and always dry-run your code with edge cases before saying you're finished.

text:
Beyond the standard DSA rounds, there was a heavy emphasis on backend architecture. I was
asked to design a globally distributed rate-limiting service. We spent significant time discussing
the trade-offs between using a centralized Redis store versus local in-memory caching to
reduce latency. The coding rounds used Go and focused on concurrency primitives like
channels and mutexes to build a thread-safe task scheduler. The interviewer pushed me on
"thundering herd" problems and ensuring graceful shutdowns of worker pools. Takeaway:
Focus on low-level system concepts and how they scale. Knowing how a database handles
locking under high load or how gRPC handles streaming was a recurring theme. Don't just
provide a working solution; provide the most optimized one for a billion users.

text:
The interview consisted of a technical screen followed by five onsite rounds. It wasn't just about
building models; there was a massive focus on A/B testing and product intuition. I was asked to
explain how to measure the success of a new UI feature in Search using specific metrics like
CTR and session length. Technical rounds covered the math behind Bayes' Theorem, linear
regression assumptions, and a SQL round with complex joins and window functions to find the
"Top N users per category." Takeaway: Don't neglect basic stats and the "Why" behind the
math. Being able to explain P-values or the bias-variance tradeoff to a non-technical
stakeholder is a common behavioral-technical hybrid check.

text:
The Online Assessment (OA) had two medium-level DSA questions and a workplace
simulation. The onsite was three rounds. Every single round started with 20 minutes of
"Leadership Principles" (LPs) questions, which are taken very seriously. Technical questions
were standard but required high accuracy—think "Top K Frequent Elements" and "LRU Cache
implementation." I had to explain my design using Object-Oriented principles, ensuring
encapsulation and modularity. Takeaway: You cannot over-prepare for LPs. Use the STAR
method for every behavioral answer and tie it back to "Customer Obsession" or "Ownership." If
your code is perfect but you fail to show backbone or deliver results in your stories, you won't
get the offer.

text:
This was a 4-round virtual onsite. The System Design round focused on designing a "Flash
Sale" system that could handle millions of concurrent requests during a Black Friday event. We
spent a lot of time on database sharding, caching layers (DAX), and using message queues like
SQS to decouple the order processing service from the payment service. The coding rounds
included a hard Dynamic Programming problem related to inventory optimization. Takeaway:
Focus on horizontal scaling and failure recovery. Amazon loves it when you mention "retry logic
with exponential backoff" and "idempotency keys" for payment APIs. Always tie your design
decisions back to the user's experience—latency is a dealbreaker.

text:
The interview rounds focused on building scalable ETL pipelines. I was given a scenario where
raw log data was arriving in S3 in various formats and had to design a schema for a star-
schema warehouse in Redshift. The SQL round was intense, involving recursive CTEs and
heavy window functions to calculate year-over-year growth per category. We also discussed
data quality checks and how to handle late-arriving data. Takeaway: Know the difference
between OLTP and OLAP inside out. Understanding when to use partitioning versus sorting
keys in Redshift is a major plus. Being able to visualize the data flow from source to dashboard
is crucial.

text:
Microsoft's process felt very "developer-centric" and collaborative. Rounds involved coding on a
shared editor focusing on clean code, proper variable naming, and modularity. Questions were
mostly around Binary Trees (e.g., "Serialize and Deserialize a Binary Tree") and String
manipulation using Stacks. There was also a unique "Debugging" round, where I had to find a
logical error in a provided snippet related to multi-threading. Takeaway: They value candidates
who write readable, maintainable code over "clever" one-liners. Don't use single-letter variables
like i  or j  unless they are loop counters. Mentioning familiarity with Azure services or C#
features like LINQ can give you an edge, even if the role is language-agnostic.

This was a heavy deep-dive into NLP and Transformer architectures. I was asked to walk
through the "Self-Attention" mechanism in detail, explaining why we use scaling factors in dot-

algorithms like Adam versus SGD. We also discussed "Model Explainability" (SHAP/LIME) for a

calculus and linear algebra, not just how to call a library. Be prepared to derive basic loss
functions on a whiteboard and discuss the trade-offs of different activation functions in deep
networks.

text:
Meta is famous for its high-speed coding expectations. You typically have 45 minutes to solve
two LeetCode-style problems perfectly. My questions were "Lowest Common Ancestor of a
Binary Tree" and a variation of "Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum." There was zero room for
logical errors. The System Design round focused on "Designing the Instagram News Feed,"
where we discussed pull vs. push models for updates and caching strategies for high-celebrity
accounts. Takeaway: Speed is everything. Practice until you can solve LeetCode Medium
problems in under 15 minutes. During the design round, focus on bottleneck identification—how
do you handle a user with 100 million followers? Scaling for "Big Data" is the only thing that
matters here.
